title: The last reader of a forgotten shorthand
summary: Narrate the story of an old clerk who is the last person able to read a dying shorthand system, and reflect on what is lost when a skill outlives its use, using past tenses, present perfect, and reflection.
topic: What happens when one person is the last who can read a once-common script?
prompt: Write a 3-paragraph narrative-reflection (400–500 words) about Mr Okafor, a retired court clerk who is the last person in his city able to read Gregg shorthand, a fast handwriting system once used by every secretary. Describe how a museum asks him to translate a box of old notebooks. Tell what he finds, then reflect on what is lost when a skill outlives the people who needed it. Use the past simple, the present perfect, and a moment of reflection. End with a thought, not a moral.
hintStarters: When the museum first phoned him, Mr Okafor assumed they had the wrong number, because…
He had not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years, and yet his hand still remembered…
What surprised him most was not the speed of the old notes but…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một thư ký đã nghỉ hưu
một kỹ năng đang mai một
một cuốn sổ đã phai màu
tồn tại lâu hơn mục đích của nó
một ghi chép về quá khứ
một nhân chứng của lịch sử
người cuối cùng thuộc loại của mình
lại cảm thấy mình có ích
một dòng liên tục không gián đoạn

sampleText:
When the museum first phoned him, Mr Okafor assumed they had the wrong number. He had spent thirty years as a court clerk in Enugu, writing down the words of witnesses in Gregg shorthand as fast as they spoke, but he had retired in 1998, and nobody had asked him about shorthand since. The young curator explained that they had found a box of notebooks in the basement of the old courthouse. The pages were covered in loops and lines that nobody on the staff could read. A historian had told them there was perhaps one man left in the city who could, and his name was Okafor.

He took the box home on the bus, holding it on his knees like something fragile. He had not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years, and yet when he uncapped his pen and traced the first curl of ink, his hand still remembered before his mind did. The symbols came back the way a song does. He worked for three weeks at his kitchen table. The notebooks turned out to be the records of small disputes from the 1950s: a quarrel over a goat, a disagreement about a fence, a woman asking the court to make her husband return her cooking pots. None of it was important. All of it was ordinary life, written down by a clerk like him and then forgotten in a basement for sixty years.

What surprised him most was not the speed of the old notes but the loneliness of being able to read them. He has thought about that loneliness every day since. He realised that he was the last bridge between those pages and the present, and that when he died, the loops would become silent again — not lost, exactly, but locked, like a letter in a language no one alive could speak. He has trained two of his grandchildren a little, on quiet Sunday afternoons, though he knows they are learning it as a curiosity rather than a craft. The court no longer needs a fast hand; a recorder does the work now, and does it better. Still, he keeps teaching. He is not trying to save the system, which deserves to rest. He is trying to keep an unbroken line, however thin, between the woman who wanted her pots back and someone in the future who might wonder what she said. A skill, he thinks, does not really die when it stops being useful. It dies on the day the last person who loved it stops passing it on.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to show an earlier past inside a story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He had not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years, and yet… his hand still remembered' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành ('had not opened') để chỉ một hành động xảy ra trước thời điểm chính trong câu chuyện (quá khứ đơn 'remembered').
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He had not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years, and yet… his hand still remembered' uses the past perfect ('had not opened') to mark an action earlier than the main past moment of the story (the past simple 'remembered').

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for a result that still matters now
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He has thought about that loneliness every day since' và 'He has trained two of his grandchildren' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nối quá khứ với hiện tại — hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn còn liên quan đến lúc kể chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He has thought about that loneliness every day since' and 'He has trained two of his grandchildren' use the present perfect to link past and present — the action began in the past and still matters at the moment of telling.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi viện bảo tàng gọi điện lần đầu, ông Okafor cho rằng họ đã gọi nhầm số. Ông đã làm thư ký tòa án ở Enugu suốt ba mươi năm, ghi lại lời của các nhân chứng bằng tốc ký Gregg nhanh như họ nói, nhưng ông đã nghỉ hưu năm 1998, và không ai hỏi ông về tốc ký kể từ đó. Người phụ trách trẻ giải thích rằng họ đã tìm thấy một hộp sổ tay trong tầng hầm của tòa án cũ. Các trang giấy phủ đầy những nét vòng và đường thẳng mà không ai trong nhân viên đọc được. Một nhà sử học đã nói với họ rằng có lẽ chỉ còn một người đàn ông trong thành phố có thể đọc được, và tên ông là Okafor.

Ông mang hộp về nhà trên xe buýt, đặt nó trên đầu gối như một thứ gì đó dễ vỡ. Ông đã không mở một cuốn sổ tốc ký nào trong hơn hai mươi năm, và thế mà khi ông mở nắp bút và vẽ nét mực cong đầu tiên, bàn tay ông vẫn còn nhớ trước cả tâm trí của ông. Các ký hiệu quay trở lại theo cách một bài hát quay lại. Ông làm việc ba tuần ở bàn bếp. Những cuốn sổ hóa ra là các ghi chép về những tranh chấp nhỏ từ những năm 1950: một cuộc cãi vã về con dê, một bất đồng về hàng rào, một người phụ nữ xin tòa buộc chồng trả lại những cái nồi nấu ăn của bà. Không có gì trong đó là quan trọng. Tất cả đều là cuộc sống thường ngày, được ghi lại bởi một thư ký như ông và rồi bị lãng quên trong tầng hầm suốt sáu mươi năm.

Điều khiến ông ngạc nhiên nhất không phải là tốc độ của những ghi chú cũ mà là nỗi cô đơn của việc có thể đọc được chúng. Ông đã nghĩ về nỗi cô đơn đó mỗi ngày kể từ đó. Ông nhận ra rằng ông là cây cầu cuối cùng giữa những trang giấy đó và hiện tại, và rằng khi ông qua đời, những nét vòng sẽ lại trở nên câm lặng — không hẳn là mất đi, mà là bị khóa lại, như một lá thư bằng một ngôn ngữ không ai còn sống có thể nói. Ông đã dạy hai đứa cháu của mình một chút, vào những buổi chiều Chủ nhật yên tĩnh, dù ông biết chúng học nó như một sự tò mò chứ không phải một nghề. Tòa án không còn cần một bàn tay nhanh nữa; một máy ghi âm làm công việc đó bây giờ, và làm tốt hơn. Tuy vậy, ông vẫn tiếp tục dạy. Ông không cố cứu hệ thống đó, thứ xứng đáng được nghỉ ngơi. Ông đang cố giữ một dòng liên tục không gián đoạn, dù mỏng manh đến đâu, giữa người phụ nữ muốn lấy lại những cái nồi và một ai đó trong tương lai có thể tự hỏi bà đã nói gì. Một kỹ năng, ông nghĩ, không thực sự chết khi nó ngừng có ích. Nó chết vào ngày người cuối cùng yêu nó ngừng truyền lại nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly to show an earlier past action?
options: He has not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years before.
He had not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years.
He did not opened a shorthand notebook in over twenty years.
He had not open a shorthand notebook in over twenty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had not opened' shows an action completed before the main past moment of the story. Option B mixes present perfect with 'before' awkwardly in a past narrative. Option C uses 'did not opened' (wrong — base verb after 'did'). Option D uses 'open' instead of the participle 'opened'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = had + past participle, even in the negative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to connect a past action to now?
options: He has thinking about that loneliness every day since.
He thought about that loneliness every day since.
He has thought about that loneliness every day since.
He had thought about that loneliness every day since.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'has thought' links the past to the present moment of telling. Option B uses 'thinking' instead of the participle. Option C uses simple past, cutting the link to now. Option D uses past perfect, which would need an even later past reference point.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = has + past participle for a result that still matters now.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the notebooks at the time he received them?
options: The pages were covered by loops that nobody could read.
The pages were covering in loops that nobody could read.
The pages were covered in loops that nobody could readed.
The pages were covered in loops that nobody could read.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Covered in' is the natural collocation for marks across a surface. Option B uses 'covered by', which suggests a single covering object. Option C uses 'covering' (active) where a passive is needed. Option D invents 'readed' instead of 'read'.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'covered in' for markings spread over a surface.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as fast as' correctly to compare two speeds?
options: He wrote the words as fast as they spoke.
He wrote the words as fast than they spoke.
He wrote the words so fast as they spoke.
He wrote the words as faster as they spoke.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As + adjective/adverb + as' compares equal speeds. Option B uses 'than', which belongs with comparatives. Option C uses 'so … as', non-standard in the affirmative. Option D uses the comparative 'faster' inside an 'as … as' frame.
mcQuestions.hint: Equal comparison uses 'as … as' with the base form of the adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best describes his hand remembering before his mind?
options: His hand still remembered before his mind was.
His hand still remembered before his mind did.
His hand still remembering before his mind did.
His hand still remembered before his mind do.
mcQuestions.explanation: The auxiliary 'did' correctly stands in for 'remembered' in the past. Option B uses 'was', which does not match the verb 'remembered'. Option C uses 'remembering' without an auxiliary. Option D uses present 'do' in a past sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Use the past auxiliary 'did' to echo the past verb 'remembered'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'turn out to be' correctly?
options: The notebooks turned out being records of small disputes.
The notebooks turned out to been records of small disputes.
The notebooks turned out to be records of small disputes.
The notebooks turned out be records of small disputes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Turn out to be' is the fixed pattern. Option B uses 'being' instead of 'to be'. Option C uses 'been' after 'to'. Option D drops the verb 'be' after 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom is 'turn out to be + noun'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally expresses that the skill should be allowed to fade?
options: the system, which deserves resting
the system, which deserve to rest
the system, that deserves to rest,
the system, which deserves to rest
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deserves to rest' uses the correct verb form and agreement, with a non-defining relative 'which'. Option B uses 'resting' after 'deserves'. Option C uses 'deserve' (wrong agreement). Option D uses 'that' in a non-defining clause, which is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Deserve to + base verb', and use 'which' (with commas) for extra information.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'however' inside a phrase meaning 'no matter how'?
options: He wants to keep an unbroken line, however thin it is.
He wants to keep an unbroken line, however thin is it.
He wants to keep an unbroken line, how ever thin it is.
He wants to keep an unbroken line, however thinner it is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However + adjective + subject + verb' means 'no matter how'. Option B inverts to 'is it' incorrectly. Option C splits 'however' into two words. Option D uses the comparative 'thinner', which does not fit.
mcQuestions.hint: 'However + adjective' keeps normal word order: subject then verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the writer's closing idea about when a skill dies?
options: It dies on the day the last person who loved it stop passing it on.
It dies on the day the last person who loved it stops passing it on.
It dies on the day the last person who loved it stops to pass it on.
It dies on the day the last person who loved it stops passing on it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stops passing it on' uses 'stop + gerund' and the natural particle order. Option B uses 'stop' without agreement. Option C uses 'stops to pass', which changes the meaning to 'pauses in order to pass'. Option D separates the phrasal verb awkwardly as 'passing on it'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Stop + gerund' means 'no longer do it'; keep 'pass it on' in its natural order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the recorder replacing him?
options: A recorder does the work now, and do it better.
A recorder makes the work now, and does it better.
A recorder does the work now, and does it better.
A recorder does the work now, and does better it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Do the work' is the correct collocation and 'does it better' keeps natural word order. Option B uses 'do' without agreement. Option C uses 'makes the work', which is non-native. Option D misorders 'better it'.
mcQuestions.hint: We 'do work', not 'make work', and the adverb 'better' comes after the object.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'realise that' correctly with a past tense?
options: He realised that he is the last bridge between those pages and the present.
He realised what he was the last bridge between those pages and the present.
He realised that he were the last bridge between those pages and the present.
He realised that he was the last bridge between those pages and the present.
mcQuestions.explanation: After past 'realised', the reported clause shifts to past 'was'. Option B keeps present 'is', breaking the sequence of tenses. Option C uses 'what' instead of 'that'. Option D uses 'were' with a singular subject.
mcQuestions.hint: After a past reporting verb, the reported verb usually shifts back to the past too.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Okafor decides to keep teaching a skill that machines now do better, not to save it but to keep a thin line of memory unbroken. Are there skills in your own family or culture that no longer have a practical use but are still worth passing on? Who decides when something is allowed to be forgotten?
